title: Capacity Planning & Resource Forecasting for Staff+ Engineers
subtitle: How to predict infrastructure needs, avoid outages, and optimize cloud spend — using data, not gut feel

sections.content:
## Why Capacity Planning Is a Staff+ Skill

At SAP, capacity planning is often static: buy hardware based on peak load, over-provision by 50%, refresh every 3 years. At FAANG scale, this approach is financially impossible. A 10% over-provision on a $100M infrastructure budget is $10M waste.

Capacity planning at FAANG is a **continuous, data-driven process** that balances:

- **Performance** — Can the system handle peak traffic without degrading?
- **Cost** — Are we paying for capacity we don't need?
- **Risk** — What happens if traffic spikes 5x above forecast?
- **Velocity** — Can we add capacity faster than traffic grows?

As a Staff+ engineer, you'll be responsible for capacity planning for your team's services. Getting it wrong means either wasted budget or a preventable outage.

### The Cost of Getting It Wrong

| Scenario | Under-provisioning | Over-provisioning |
|---|---|---|
| Impact | Outage, degraded performance, SLO breach, revenue loss | Wasted spend, reduced R&D budget, lower margins |
| Example | AWS outage during Prime Day (est. $100M lost sales) | Orphaned cloud resources (avg 30% of cloud spend is waste) |
| Detection | Immediate (alerts fire) | Gradual (never noticed until cost review) |
| Fix | Emergency scaling (slow, expensive) | Decommission (low urgency, rarely done) |
| FAANG philosophy | Prefer over-provisioning for user-facing services | Prefer under-provisioning for batch/non-critical services |

cards.title: Time-Series Forecasting
cards.body:
The most common approach. Use historical traffic data to predict future needs.

**Simple methods:**
- Linear regression: traffic = base + growth_rate * time
- Exponential smoothing: weight recent data more heavily
- Holt-Winters: captures trend + seasonality (daily, weekly, yearly)

**Advanced (FAANG-scale):**
- Prophet (Meta): Handles holidays, changepoints, and missing data
- SARIMA: Seasonal ARIMA for strong periodic patterns
- ML-based: XGBoost/LightGBM with features (day-of-week, hour, marketing events, product launches)

**Key principle:** Forecast at the service level, sum up for total. Don't forecast total infrastructure spend directly — it masks individual service trends.

cards.title: Leading Indicators
cards.body:
Leading indicators predict capacity needs before demand hits:

**User growth metrics:**
- New user signups (leading by 30-90 days)
- DAU/MAU growth rate
- Marketing campaign pipeline

**Product signals:**
- New feature launch calendar (each feature has an expected traffic multiplier)
- API consumer growth (more integrations = more traffic)
- Data volume growth (more users = more data = more storage demand)

**Business inputs:**
- Quarterly OKRs and growth targets
- Expansion into new regions/markets
- Enterprise customer commitments (contractually guaranteed throughput)

**FAANG practice:** Monthly 'capacity review' with product, engineering, and finance. Each team presents 3-month ahead forecast with confidence intervals.

cards.title: Load Testing & Benchmarking
cards.body:
Forecasts predict traffic volume, but load testing determines how much capacity each unit of traffic consumes.

**Key metrics per service:**
- Requests per second per CPU core
- Memory per concurrent connection
- Disk IOPS per write operation
- Network bandwidth per request

**Scaling law:** Does the service scale linearly? Or does throughput degrade at high concurrency (Amdahl's law)?

**How to measure:**
- Production shadow traffic (duplicate live traffic to a test instance)
- Canary analysis (ramp up traffic to a new instance until it breaks)
- Synthetic load testing (Gatling, k6, Locust) at 2x, 5x, 10x peak

cards.title: Reserved vs On-Demand Strategy
cards.body:
Cloud capacity comes in three flavors with different economics:

**Reserved/Committed (1-3 year):** 30-60% discount vs on-demand. Best for predictable base load (40-60% of total traffic).

**On-Demand:** Full price, infinite elasticity. Best for spikes and unpredictable traffic.

**Spot/Preemptible:** 60-90% discount, but can be reclaimed. Best for fault-tolerant workloads (batch processing, CI/CD, stateless workers).

**The Staff+ strategy:**
1. Forecast base load (P50 traffic) → reserved capacity
2. Forecast peak load (P99 traffic) → on-demand autoscaling
3. Batch/fault-tolerant work → spot
4. Buffer: 20% headroom above P99 forecast for unexpected spikes

sections.title: Autoscaling Strategies
rows: Time of day / day of week
Known periodic patterns (batch jobs, business hours)

sections.title: Capacity Planning Worksheet (Staff+ Template)
sections.code:
-- Capacity Planning for 'User Profile Service' --

1. TRAFFIC FORECAST (next 12 months)
   Current DAU: 5M
   Growth rate: 15% QoQ (from product OKRs)
   New feature impact: 'AI Profile Photo' launches Q2, expected +20% traffic
   
   Month | DAU | Requests/s | Peak Requests/s (2x avg)
   ------|-----|------------|---------------------
   Jan   | 5M  | 5,800      | 11,600
   Apr   | 6.6M| 7,600      | 15,200  (new feature)
   Jul   | 7.6M| 8,700      | 17,400
   Oct   | 8.7M| 10,000     | 20,000
   Dec   | 10M | 11,600     | 23,200  (holiday peak)

2. RESOURCE PER REQUEST (from load testing)
   CPU: 50ms per request = 50,000 req/s per CPU core
   Memory: 2MB per concurrent connection
   Storage: 10KB per new profile = 50GB/month growth
   Network: 100KB per response (includes profile photo)

3. CAPACITY REQUIRED
   Month | CPU Cores | Memory (GB) | Storage (GB) | Network (Gbps)
   ------|-----------|-------------|--------------|---------------
   Jan   | 12        | 48          | 500          | 10
   Apr   | 16        | 64          | 700          | 13
   Jul   | 18        | 72          | 900          | 15
   Oct   | 20        | 80          | 1,100        | 17
   Dec   | 24        | 96          | 1,400        | 20

4. COST PROJECTION (monthly)
   Reserved (60% of peak): 14 cores x $30 = $420
   On-demand (40% of peak): 10 cores x $60 = $600
   Storage: 1,400GB x $0.10 = $140
   Network: 20Gbps x $50 = $1,000
   Total Dec: $2,160/month
   Total Annual: ~$18,000

5. RISK SCENARIOS
   Worst case: Viral launch drives 10x traffic -> $12K/month emergency spend
   Mitigation: Request throttling + CDN cache (absorbs 5x before scaling needed)

sections.content:
## SAP Bridge — Capacity Planning in the SAP World

### Current SAP Practice

SAP capacity planning is typically done during implementation:

- **SAP Quick Sizer** — Questionnaire-based tool that estimates HANA memory, CPU, and disk based on expected data volume and user count. Results in a 'sizing' that maps to certified hardware.
- **Static sizing** — Once sized, the system runs on fixed hardware for 3-5 years until the next upgrade.
- **HANA memory is the constraint** — HANA is an in-memory database. If data grows beyond memory, performance degrades catastrophically (column store falls back to disk).

### The FAANG Approach Applied to SAP

| Aspect | SAP Traditional | FAANG-Inspired |
|---|---|---|
| Sizing method | Quick Sizer (one-time) | Continuous forecasting (monthly) |
| Capacity buffer | 50% over-provision (safety) | 20% headroom + autoscaling |
| Scaling model | Vertical (buy bigger hardware) | Horizontal (add more nodes) |
| Cost awareness | Capital expense (CAPEX) | Operating expense (OPEX, hourly) |
| Forecasting inputs | Questionnaire answers | Historical usage + business OKRs |
| Risk management | Over-provision to be safe | Reserved + on-demand mix |

### Practical Guidance for SAP Architects

1. **HANA Cloud changes the game** — With HANA Cloud on AWS/Azure/GCP, you can scale compute and storage independently. This is the first SAP database that supports FAANG-style elasticity.
2. **Monitor actual usage, not Quick Sizer** — Quick Sizer estimates are notoriously inaccurate (often 2-3x over actual). Use SAP Cloud ALM or Prometheus to measure actual CPU/memory/IOPS.
3. **Plan for data growth** — HANA's in-memory nature means storage growth directly affects capacity. Forecast data volume separately from compute.
4. **The 'HANA memory wall'** — When a HANA instance runs out of memory, it doesn't degrade gracefully. Your capacity plan must include a safety margin above peak data volume.

sections.title: Capacity Planning FAQ for Staff+ Interviews

tabs.label: Methodology
tabs.content:
**Interviewer:** "How would you forecast capacity needs for a new feature?"

**Staff+ answer:** "I'd use a **bottom-up + top-down hybrid** approach:

**Top-down:** Product says the feature targets 5M DAU by end of year. That's the envelope.

**Bottom-up:** I estimate per-user traffic: each user sends 10 requests/day for this feature = 50M requests/day = 580 req/s average, 1,200 req/s peak.

Then I look at similar existing features for resource-per-request. If the 'feed' feature uses 30ms CPU and 1MB memory per request, that's a starting point. I load-test the new feature in a staging environment to get accurate numbers.

**Result:** Feature will need 36 CPU cores and 72GB memory at peak. Cost: ~$3,000/month. Growth rate: matches DAU growth (15% QoQ).

**The confidence interval matters:** P50 forecast: 1,200 req/s. P90: 2,000 req/s (if feature is more popular than expected). P99: 5,000 req/s (if it goes viral). I plan for P90, have budget authority to scale to P99."

**Why this impresses:** You combined two forecasting methods, used data from similar features, included load testing validation, and gave a confidence interval.

tabs.label: Handling Spikes
tabs.content:
**Interviewer:** "Your service normally handles 10K req/s. A marketing campaign is expected to drive 50K req/s for 2 hours. How do you prepare?"

**Staff+ answer:** "Four strategies, in order of preference:

**1. Pre-scale** — 24 hours before the campaign, scale the service to 50K capacity. Keep it running through the event. Cost: 2 hours x 5x capacity = negligible. Risk: zero. This is the FAANG default for planned events.

**2. Request throttling + queueing** — Set up a request queue (SQS, Kafka) with a 5-minute TTL. If traffic exceeds 50K, excess requests queue. The frontend polls for results. Works for async features (not real-time).

**3. CDN + caching** — If the campaign page is cacheable, push a static version to CDN. Most users see cached content. Only uncacheable API calls hit the backend.

**4. Degraded mode** — If all else fails, turn off non-critical features (personalization, recommendations) and serve a simpler response. Users get a functional but not personalized experience.

**Capability to implement:** Autoscaling with predictive scaling (ML model predicts the 50K spike based on campaign schedule). Without that, schedule-based scaling (cron job at campaign start)."

**Why this impresses:** You gave four concrete strategies in priority order, with cost/risk analysis for each, and an implementation path (predictive vs scheduled scaling).

tabs.label: SAP Scenario
tabs.content:
**Interviewer:** "SAP HANA Cloud is running out of memory. How do you handle it?"

**Staff+ answer:** "HANA running out of memory is a critical incident. Three-phase response:

**Phase 1: Immediate (minutes)** — Identify what's consuming memory. Use HANA's memory consumption monitors (M_MEMORY, M_HEAP_MEMORY). Is it column store data growth, a runaway query, or a session leak? Kill the offending session or query. If it's data growth, truncate or archive the largest unused tables.

**Phase 2: Tactical (hours)** — Scale up the HANA Cloud instance (more memory). HANA Cloud supports online scaling. Add memory, wait for column store to re-load into memory. This buys time.

**Phase 3: Strategic (weeks)** — Implement a capacity plan:
- Forecast data growth (which tables grow fastest?)
- Archive old data (HANA Data Tiering Optimization, BW nearline storage)
- Partition the largest tables (distribute data across more nodes)
- Set up HANA memory alerts at 70%, 80%, 90% usage
- Budget for quarterly capacity reviews

**The key lesson:** Memory pressure on HANA is a slow-moving problem (data grows gradually). The solution is a monthly capacity review, not an emergency response."

**Why this impresses:** You gave a three-phase response (immediate/tactical/strategic) with specific SAP tools (M_MEMORY, Data Tiering Optimization, online scaling). Shows you understand both the technical and operational aspects of HANA capacity management.

tabs.label: Cost Optimization
tabs.content:
**Interviewer:** "Your team's cloud bill doubled. How do you investigate and optimize?"

**Staff+ answer:** "Systematic approach:

**1. Break down by service** — Which services grew fastest? Use AWS Cost Explorer or GCP Billing with service-level tags. Identify the top 3 cost drivers.

**2. Break down by resource type** — Is it compute, storage, network, or database? Each has different optimization strategies.

**3. Analyze the growth pattern** — Did traffic grow proportionally? If compute grew 2x but traffic grew 1.5x, there's an efficiency problem.

**Common findings:**
- **Orphaned resources:** 30% of cloud resources are unused (test environments, stale snapshots). Tag governance + automated deletion fixes this.
- **Over-provisioned instances:** Services running on large instances that could use smaller ones with autoscaling.
- **No reserved capacity:** Paying on-demand prices for base load that hasn't changed in 6 months.
- **Data hoarding:** Storing data in hot storage (S3 Standard) that should be in cold (S3 Glacier Infrequent Access).
- **Cross-region data transfer:** Services in different regions talking to each other without reason.

**The FAANG approach:** Monthly 'cost review' where each service owner presents their cost-per-transaction trend. A rising trend triggers an investigation. A flat or falling trend is green."

**Why this impresses:** You gave a systematic investigation methodology (breakdown by service, resource, growth pattern), enumerated the top 5 optimization opportunities with specific percentages, and framed it as an ongoing process (monthly cost review) rather than a one-time cleanup.

cards.title: Forecast at Service Level
cards.body: Total infrastructure forecast masks individual service trends. Forecast each service independently, then sum for total. Use time-series methods (Prophet, SARIMA) or ML.

cards.title: Confidence Intervals, Not Point Estimates
cards.body: A single number for future capacity is wrong. Give P50/P90/P99 ranges. Plan for P90, have authority to scale to P99. Use confidence intervals in every forecast.

cards.title: Load Test to Get Resource-Per-Request
cards.body: Traffic forecasts are useless without resource-per-request data. Load test every service to know CPU/memory/IOPS per request. Update benchmarks quarterly.

cards.title: Reserved + On-Demand + Spot Mix
cards.body: Reserve 40-60% of base load (30-60% discount). Use on-demand for spikes. Use spot for batch/fault-tolerant. Never run everything on-demand.

cards.title: Autoscaling Isn't Instant
cards.body: Even the fastest autoscaler takes 1-5 minutes. For flash crowds (marketing events, viral launches), pre-scale or use predictive scaling.

cards.title: Monthly Cost Review
cards.body: Capacity planning is a continuous process, not a one-time exercise. Monthly review of cost-per-transaction, forecast accuracy, and optimization opportunities.
